content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. Construct an array PPP of size NNN such that:


PPP is a permutation of the first NNN natural numbers, i.e, each integer 1,2,3,…N1, 2, 3, \ldots N1,2,3,…N occurs exactly once in PPP. For example, [1,3,2][1, 3, 2][1,3,2] is a permutation of size 333, but [1,3,4][1, 3, 4][1,3,4] and [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2] are not.
PPP is **indivisible**. PPP is said to be **indivisible** if iii does not divide PiP_iPi​ for every index 2≤i≤N2 \leq i \leq N2≤i≤N.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line with N space‑separated integers forming an indivisible permutation of 1…N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each N we output N followed by 1..N‑1. Since for every i≥2 we have P_i = i‑1 < i, i cannot divide P_i, so the permutation is indivisible.

solution.approach: A simple cyclic right shift works. Place N at position 1 and for every i (2 ≤ i ≤ N) put i‑1 at position i. Then for each i ≥ 2 we have P_i = i‑1 < i, so i cannot divide P_i. The array is a permutation because all numbers 1…N appear exactly once.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(sum of N).
